card.intuition: An approach to web design that makes web pages render well on a variety of devices and window or screen sizes from minimum to maximum display size.
card.analogy: Water in a container: on a narrow phone, the content (water) flows into a tall, thin shape. On a wide desktop, it flows into a flat, wide shape. The content is the same, but the 'container' (viewport) forces it to rearrange.
when_to_use: Making a website usable on Mobile, Tablet, and Desktop
Hiding non-essential sidebar content on smaller screens
Switching from a 3-column layout to a 1-column list for readability
gotchas: Always design 'Mobile First': write styles for small screens first, then use media queries to ADD complexity for larger screens
Don't forget the Meta Viewport tag in your HTML; without it, mobile browsers will just zoom out and make everything tiny

steps.label: Viewport
steps.annotation: Set <meta name="viewport"> — tells mobile to use device width.

steps.label: Fluid layout
steps.annotation: Use %, rem, vw instead of fixed px — layout scales naturally.

steps.label: Breakpoints
steps.annotation: Media queries apply styles at specific viewport width thresholds.

steps.label: Images
steps.annotation: Responsive images: provide multiple sizes, browser picks optimal.

steps.label: Test
steps.annotation: Test at common breakpoints — mobile-first approach recommended.

exercise.prompt: Identify the CSS rule used to apply styles only when the screen is wider than 768px.
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() == "media"
assert check("media")
print("PASS")
